title: Lathyrus armenus
intro: Lathyrus armenus, also known as Armenian pea or Armenian vetch, is a perennial legume native to the mountainous regions of Turkey and Armenia. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including anemia, inflammation, and respiratory issues, due to its rich content of nutrients, antioxidants, and bioactive compounds. The bioactive constituents of Lathyrus armenus include isoflavones, saponins, and phenolic acids, which have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-cancer properties. The plant is commonly used to make herbal teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being used in traditional medicine to treat conditions such as bronchitis, coughs, and skin disorders.
taxonomy: Lathyrus armenus is a species within the genus Lathyrus, which belongs to the family Fabaceae, commonly known as legumes. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics, such as its pea-like flowers and compound leaves. The genus Lathyrus is further divided into several species, each with unique features. Lathyrus armenus is a member of the section Lathyrus, which includes species primarily found in the Old World. The classification of Lathyrus armenus reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the Fabaceae family.

side_effects: Lathyrus armenus, also known as the Armenian pea, contains a toxin called ODAP (oxalyldiaminopropionic acid) that can be toxic to humans if consumed in large quantities. If ingested, ODAP can cause a range of side effects, including neurolathyrism, a condition characterized by muscle spasms, paralysis, and other neurological problems. Consuming large amounts of the plant can also lead to liver and kidney damage, as well as other systemic issues. The toxic effects of Lathyrus armenus can be more severe in individuals with pre-existing medical conditions or those who are malnourished. Pregnant women and children are particularly susceptible to the toxic effects of the plant.
